prompt:
I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. He's hoping to get into a competitive writing program with something like this. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.
thinking:
The user wants me to grade a short personal narrative essay written by a student hoping to get into a competitive writing program.
The topic: "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned."
Length target: ~500 words.
Output needed: Grade out of 10, brief feedback on what works and what doesn't.

**Analysis of the essay:**

*   **Content/Tone:** The story is relatable and honest. It follows a clear narrative arc: hubris (driveway dominance), the event (tryouts), the low point (scrimmage comment), the aftermath (cut, realization), the pivot (debate team), and the reflection (lessons learned). The reflection is solid—acknowledging the Dunning-Kruger effect essentially ("you don't know what you don't know") without using the jargon.
*   **Style/Voice:** The voice is casual, almost too casual for a *competitive writing program* application. Phrases like "Obviously," "So yeah," and "I figured making the team would be no problem" feel conversational. While conversational isn't bad, for a writing program, one usually expects a bit more polish, stronger vocabulary, or a more distinct stylistic flair.
*   **Structure:** The structure is logical. Introduction, rising action, climax, falling action, conclusion. It meets the prompt requirements directly.
*   **Specific Strengths:**
    *   Self-awareness is high. The student admits they were annoying ("I could tell they were annoyed with me") and that the mean kid wasn't wrong.
    *   The ending insight ("failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information") is a strong closer.
    *   The transition to debate is handled well—it connects the failure to the success.
*   **Specific Weaknesses:**
    *   **Sentence Variation:** The sentences are somewhat repetitive in structure (Subject-Verb). "I had been playing...", "My friend Marcus and I would play...", "The tryouts were...", "I realized...", "The other kids were...", "I didn't know...", "When the coach asked...", "I had to watch...", "I still got it wrong." It reads a bit staccato and monotonous.
    *   **Vocabulary:** Very basic. "Fast and taller," "pretty good," "totally different things," "hard because," "upset," "weird pivot." There's little imagery or sensory detail beyond the basics.
    *   **Mechanics/Grammar:** Generally clean, though very simple.
    *   **"Show, Don't Tell":** The essay leans heavily on telling. "I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league." vs describing the feeling of the ball being stolen, the sweat, the confusion. "I was really upset." vs describing the mood at home.
    *   **Suitability for Competitive Writing Program:** This is the key context. Is this "competitive writing program" level? Probably not for a top-tier creative writing program. It's a solid B/A- high school essay, but for a portfolio piece or application essay, it lacks artistry. It's functional but not evocative.

**Scoring Rationale:**
If this were a standard English class assignment (9th grade), it's a solid 7 or 8.
However, the prompt specifies: "He's hoping to get into a competitive writing program."
For a competitive writing program, the bar is higher. They want to see potential, command of language, and a unique voice.
*   It's safe and competent but uninspired.
*   The voice is generic teen.
*   The prose is workmanlike but lacks rhythm or poetic flair.
*   Therefore, the score should reflect the specific context. A 5/10 or 6/10 seems appropriate for a *competitive program* context. It's a "pass" in terms of clarity, but a "fail" in terms of demonstrating high-level writing potential.

Let's refine the score.
Narrative arc: 8/10 (clear)
Insight: 8/10 (good for age)
Prose/Style: 4/10 (too basic, repetitive structure for a writing program)
Mechanics: 9/10 (clean)

Overall average for a specific context: I'll go with a 5.5 or 6. Let's settle on a **6/10**. It's a "nice" story, but for a writer, the writing itself needs to be the main attraction, and here it's flat.

**Drafting Feedback:**

*   **What works:**
    *   Clear arc and structure.
    *   Honest self-reflection (especially about the "stay out of the way" comment).
    *   Good thematic conclusion (failure is just information).
*   **What doesn't:**
    *   **Sentence Fluency:** Too many sentences start with "I" or simple subjects. Needs more complex sentence structures and rhythm.
    *   **Show, Don't Tell:** Relies on summary ("I was really upset," "I was out of my league") rather than evocative details.
    *   **Voice:** Too casual/conversational. Needs more "polish" or specific stylistic flair to stand out in a competitive application.

**Refining the Grade:**
If I give a 6, the user might think it's average. For a competitive writing program, this is likely rejection material. It reads like a standard homework assignment. I need to be honest about that. A 6 is fair—it's competent execution of the prompt, but lacking the "spark" required for the specific goal.

Let's adjust the tone to be constructive but firm about the standards of a writing program.

**Final Check of the Essay:**
"The first time I really failed..." -> Cliche opening.
"My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one..." -> Functional.
"The other kids were faster and taller..." -> Telling.
"I could tell they were annoyed..." -> Telling.
"One kid said 'just stay out of the way'..." -> Good dialogue, could be expanded.
"I was really upset..." -> Telling.
"Instead I joined the debate team..." -> Good narrative beat.
"So yeah, I failed..." -> Very casual ending.

The student has the *mind* of a writer (good insight), but lacks the *craft* yet. That is a valuable distinction to make in the feedback.

**Grade:** 5/10. (Harsher because of the "competitive writing program" context. If it was just a grade 10 English essay, I'd give it a 7/10). I will stick to the context provided.

**Feedback points:**
1.  **Voice:** Too casual.
2.  **Structure/Flow:** Repetitive sentence openings.
3.  **Imagery:** Needs more sensory detail.

Let's construct the final response.

**Feedback:**

For a standard high school English assignment, this would likely receive a solid B (7 or 8/10). It is grammatically clean, follows a logical structure, and answers the prompt honestly. However, for a student hoping to enter a **competitive writing program**, this essay falls flat. It lacks the stylistic flair, command of language, and "voice" that such programs usually look for. It reads more like a journal entry than a polished piece of creative nonfiction.

**What Works:**
*   **The "Turn":** The narrative arc is solid. The transition from the failure (basketball) to the realization of a new strength (debate) is handled well and feels authentic.

*   **The Theme:** The conclusion—that failure is just "information"—is a strong, punchy thesis statement that lands well.

**What Doesn’t Work:**
*   **"Show, Don’t Tell":** The essay relies heavily on summary rather than scene. Phrases like "I was really upset" or "I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league" are *telling*. For a competitive program, the student needs to describe the physical sensation of failing—the squeak of sneakers, the specific humiliation of the drill, the heat in the face—to evoke emotion in the reader.
*   **Sentence Variation:** The writing is rhythmically repetitive. Many sentences follow a simple "Subject-Verb" structure ("I had been playing," "My friend Marcus and I would play," "The tryouts were," "The other kids were"). This creates a staccato, monotonous flow. Complex sentence structures and varied syntax are essential for showing writing prowess.
*   **Casual Tone:** The voice is too conversational. Phrases like "So yeah," "Obviously," and "weird pivot" undermine the author's authority. While a personal voice is good, it still needs to sound polished and intentional.

**Advice for the Student:**
Encourage the student to rewrite this with a focus on **sensory details**. Don't just say the other kids knew plays; describe the chaos of the court that the student couldn't decode. Don't just say they joined debate; describe the adrenaline of the first argument that made them realize they had found their arena. They have a good story; they just need to elevate the prose to match it.

Here is an assessment of the essay based on the standards of a competitive writing program.

### **Grade: 5.5 / 10**

### **Feedback**

**What Works:**
*   **The Core Insight:** The student’s realization that "you don't know what you don't know" is a sophisticated observation. Recognizing the difference between casual proficiency and competitive standards shows maturity and self-awareness.
*   **Specific Details:** The inclusion of the "pick and roll" and the specific line of dialogue ("just stay out of the way") grounds the narrative. These moments help the reader visualize the scene better than general complaints about playing poorly.
*   **Clarity:** The essay is structurally sound. It has a clear beginning, middle, and end, and answers the prompt directly.

**What Doesn’t Work (for a Competitive Program):**
*   **The Voice is Too Casual:** While the tone is authentic, it leans too heavily on conversational filler ("So yeah," "pretty much did," "totally different things"). For a competitive writing program, the prose needs to be tighter and more polished. The conversational style undermines the authority of the reflection.
*   **"Telling" Instead of "Showing":** The essay relies heavily on summarizing emotions rather than evoking them. For example, saying "I was really upset for a while" is flat. A stronger writer would describe how that failure felt physically or emotionally (e.g., the silence in the car on the ride home, or avoiding eye contact with Marcus in the hallway).
*   **Lack of Sensory Imagery:** Aside from the basketball terms, there is very little description of the setting. We don't hear the squeak of sneakers, smell the gym, or feel the exhaustion of the tryouts. The narrative takes place in a vacuum.
*   **Rushed Conclusion:** The pivot to the debate team feels abrupt. The student claims it was a "weird pivot" but doesn't explain *why* debate suited them or how the skills translated. It feels like a tacked-on happy ending rather than an organic realization.
*   **The Ending:** Starting the final paragraph with "So yeah" undermines the impact of the lesson. It sounds like the student is shrugging off the essay rather than concluding a thought-provoking narrative.

**Bottom Line:**
This is a competent essay that would likely receive an A in a standard high school English class. However, for a **competitive writing program**, it lacks the "spark," stylistic flair, and emotional depth required to stand out. It tells a story, but it doesn't transport the reader.

My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

Here is a second opinion on the essay, keeping in mind the context that the student is applying for a competitive writing program.

### **Grade: 6/10**

For a general high school English assignment, this is a solid, competent piece of writing. However, for a **competitive writing program**, it feels safe and somewhat plain. Admissions committees for these programs are looking for a distinct voice, stylistic flair, and the ability to create imagery, not just tell a story chronologically.

### **What Works**

*   **The Narrative Arc:** The story structure is logical and easy to follow. It moves clearly from the setup (the driveway confidence) to the conflict (tryouts), the climax (the "stay out of the way" comment), and the resolution (joining debate).
*   **Authentic Voice:** The voice feels genuine and grounded. It doesn’t sound like the student is trying too hard to use a thesaurus, which is a good trap to avoid. The admission "I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did" is a very relatable, human moment that shows emotional maturity.
*   **Thematic Connection:** The realization that "driveway basketball" and "competitive basketball" are different sports is a strong observation. The pivot to joining the debate team is a satisfying conclusion that shows growth and self-awareness.

### **What Doesn’t Work**

*   **"Telling" instead of "Showing":** This is the biggest issue for a creative writing applicant. The student summarizes the emotions rather than letting the reader feel them.
    *   *Example:* "I was really upset for a while."
    *   *Fix:* Describe the physical reaction. Did he rip the list off the wall? Did he throw his basketball in the trash? Did he sit in the locker room until the lights went out?
*   **Lack of Imagery and Detail:** The writing is very "reportive." We know there were drills, but we don't hear the squeak of sneakers or smell the sweat. We know the other kids were better, but we don't get a specific description of *how* they moved differently.
    *   *Example:* "The other kids were faster and taller."
    *   *Fix:* Pick one specific player. "One boy had calves like coiled springs and moved down the court like smoke."
*   **Basic Sentence Structure:** The sentences are largely simple or compound sentences joined by "and." There is little rhythmic variation. Competitive writing programs look for students who can control the flow of the language, using complex sentences and fragments for emphasis.
*   **The Conclusion:** The final paragraph is too didactic (preachy). It explains the moral of the story explicitly ("I think everyone needs at least one experience like that"). In high-level writing, it is often more powerful to end on an image or an action rather than summarizing the lesson for the reader.

### **Summary Feedback for the Student**
This is a great foundation with a clear, honest story, but it reads more like a personal statement for a college application than a piece of creative writing. To make this competitive, you need to stop being a reporter and start being a storyteller. Slow down the key moments—specifically the scrimmage and the immediate aftermath of seeing the list. Use sensory details to put us in your shoes so we feel the humiliation and the relief, rather than just reading about them.

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. He's hoping to get into a competitive writing program with something like this. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

### Grade: 6.5/10

For a standard high school English assignment, this is a solid B or B+. It answers the prompt, has a clear structure, and the grammar is clean. However, for a **competitive writing program**, the bar is much higher. Admissions officers are looking for a distinct voice, stylistic flair, and the ability to "show" rather than "tell." This essay plays it safe and relies on summarization rather than scene-setting.

### What Works

*   **The Insight (The "Why"):** The student’s takeaway is surprisingly mature. The realization that "you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious" is a sophisticated observation about the Dunning-Kruger effect (even if they don't name it). That specific nugget of wisdom is the strongest part of the piece.
*   **The Narrative Arc:** The story moves logically. It establishes the false confidence, details the failure, shows the consequence, and ends with a pivot to a new activity (debate). The connection between basketball and debate (being better at arguing than dribbling) provides a nice, cohesive thematic bow.
*   **Pacing:** The story moves quickly and doesn't get bogged down in unnecessary exposition. It respects the word count.

### What Doesn't Work

*   **"Tell" vs. "Show":** This is the biggest issue for a writing program applicant. The essay summarizes emotions rather than letting the reader feel them.
    *   *Example:* "I was really upset for a while." (Telling)
    *   *Fix:* Describe the silence in the car on the ride home, the crumpled tryout sheet in the trash, or the specific feeling of checking the list and not seeing the name.
    *   *Example:* "I could tell they were annoyed with me." (Telling)
    *   *Fix:* Describe a specific teammate rolling their eyes, huffing loudly, or refusing to pass the ball.
*   **Generic Phrasing:** The prose is functional but flat. Phrases like "out of my league," "worked out fine," and "totally different things" are conversational clichés that don't demonstrate a command of language.
*   **Voice:** The voice is very casual ("So yeah," "Obviously," "pretty much did"). While authenticity is good, this feels more like a spoken anecdote to a friend than a crafted piece of writing.
*   **The Ending:** The final paragraph ("So yeah, I failed...") weakens the impact. The lesson about failure being "information" is good, but the transition into it is abrupt and feels slightly like a moral tacked on at the end.

### Final Advice for the Student
The student has a good skeleton here—the story and the lesson are solid. To make this competitive, they need to slow down the moment of failure. They should zoom in on the "scrimmage" scene. Make the reader sweat with them. Replace the summary with sensory details (the sound of sneakers, the height of the ceiling, the specific words the mean kid said). If they can repaint the middle section with vivid imagery rather than summary, this could easily become an 8 or 9/10.

prompt:
I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. He's hoping to get into a competitive writing program with something like this. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. He's hoping to get into a competitive writing program with something like this. Can you help? The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

1.  **Delete the introduction.** Start right in the action (in media res). Start with the whistle blowing or the specific feeling of being lost on the court.
2.  **Expand the "Scene."** Spend 200 words just on the scrimmage. Describe the panic, the sweat, the specific words the teammate used.
3.  **Elevate the Vocabulary.** Replace casual phrases with more precise verbs. Instead of "I was upset," use "I was humiliated" or "I seethed."

This is a solid "B" essay for a standard English class, but it needs more stylistic flair and descriptive depth to stand out for a specialized writing program.
